1:
The whole cloud of witnesses agrees on the heart of John 18:1: when his discourse and prayer were finished, Jesus went out across the Kidron to a garden not to hide but to deliver himself willingly to his Passion. The differences among the schools are of method and emphasis, not substance.

The grammatical-historical commentators are busiest here, working the geography and text. They identify the unnamed garden as Gethsemane on the Mount of Olives (so Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott), and note that John, having found the agony fully told by the others, passes it over and marks only its place. Robertson glosses the rare word cheimarrous, a torrent that flows only in winter, usually dry. On the name itself there is a genuine textual debate: is it “the brook of the cedars” (tōn Kedrōn) or the indeclinable Hebrew “Kidron” (tou Kedrōn)? Lightfoot, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson reject the “cedars” reading and derive the name from a root meaning “black,” the dark, turbid sewer-brook of the city — a reading Calvin among the Reformed independently prefers.

The Reformed voices read the scene theologically. Calvin notes that John deliberately gathers what the others omit, and that Christ’s entering a place “well known to the traitor” proves he died not by constraint but as a voluntary sacrifice, for “without obedience atonement would not have been obtained for us.” Gill ties the verse back to the farewell discourse and high-priestly prayer just concluded.

The Latin fathers, through the Catena, press typology. Augustine begins the narrative of the Passion here and harmonizes John with the Synoptic agony; Alcuin hears in the crossed brook that Christ “drinks of the brook of his Passion,” and finds it fitting that the sin committed in a garden (Genesis 3) should be blotted out in a garden. Augustine adds the somber image of “the wolf in sheep’s clothing” permitted among the sheep.

The Greek fathers (Chrysostom, Theophylact) stress the willing midnight journey: he crosses the brook and hastens to the spot known to Judas, “lessening the labor to those who plotted against him” and showing the disciples he went freely. The Puritan Matthew Henry frames Christ as “the captain of our salvation” taking the field, and reads the crossing through David’s type — the king who passed over Kidron weeping in flight from Absalom (2 Samuel 15:23, 30) and the Messiah who “shall drink of the brook in the way” (Psalm 110:7). The Wesleyan Wesley notes the garden likely belonged to a friend, a place of secret devotion and a way to avoid a tumultuous rescue. The cross-references the commentators marshal — Matthew 26:36, Luke 22:39, John 14:31 — converge on one portrait: the willing Sufferer keeping his custom even unto death.
2:
The comment on John 18:2 is thin and almost wholly grammatical-historical and Reformed, with one shared point: that Judas knew the place not because Jesus was caught off guard but because this garden was his habitual resort, so that his presence there was deliberate self-exposure.

The grammatical-historical expositors fill in the circumstances. Barnes observes that Jesus, having no home in the city, sought this retired spot for private prayer; from this he presses a devotional application, that “every Christian should have some place — be it a grove, a room, or a garden — where he may be alone and offer his devotions to God,” the Mount of Olives being the Lord’s chosen resort during the great feasts (Luke 21:37). Clarke supplies the historical logic: with Jerusalem crowded for Passover, lodging was scarce, so Jesus regularly passed the night in the garden (compare Luke 22:39); and since he had supped in the city, Judas reasoned he would be found there rather than at Bethany, where the Lord often lodged. Ellicott marks this as one of John’s touches of “exact knowledge” of the Jerusalem life of Jesus — only he remembers that the spot, perhaps a friend’s, was the Lord’s habitual haunt, and that Judas therefore knew where to look. Robertson parses the verb “resorted” (aorist passive of synagō, “gathered together,” awkward until one supplies “with his disciples”), and reads Judas’s plan — to seize Jesus by night, alone, at his very place of prayer, during the feast — as “the very spirit of the devil.”

The Reformed Gill makes the same point doctrinally. The detail that Judas knew the place shows that Christ “did not go there to hide and secure himself” from the traitor, but to meet him and “have an opportunity of finding him with the greater ease.” Gill also notes the Evangelist’s care to distinguish this Judas “which betrayed him” from the other disciple of the same name, and that though the betrayal was not yet done, “it was determined he should, and Christ knew it.” Wesley adds only the Synoptic cross-references (Mark 14:43, Luke 22:47). Across the voices, then, the verse’s freight is uniform: the knowledge of Judas underscores the deliberate willingness of Christ to be taken.
3:
On John 18:3 the witnesses divide cleanly by labor: the grammatical-historical commentators dissect the arresting force, while the Greek and Latin fathers read the scene morally and theologically. All agree the “band” was a substantial, armed, mixed company come by night against an unarmed man.

The grammatical-historical voices fix the technical sense of speira. Ellicott and Robertson identify it as the Roman cohort — the garrison band from the Tower of Antonia — and carefully distinguish the Roman soldiers from the “officers,” the temple police sent by the chief priests and Pharisees (compare John 7:32, 45); the Authorised Version, Ellicott complains, misleads by fusing “a band of men and officers” into one clause, when the band was Roman and the officers Jewish. Clarke surveys the disputed size of the cohort from Polybius and concludes no certain number can be fixed, while Gill, more freely, supposes five hundred or more — a vast force to take one unarmed man. On the lanterns and torches they agree against necessity: it was full-moon Passover, so the lights betray a fixed determination to hunt him out of any dark corner or cavern (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson). Robertson glosses the two words for the lights (phanos, a torch; lampas, an oil lamp) and adds that Mark’s “swords and staves” suit both soldiers and temple guard.

The Latin and Greek fathers, through the Catena, moralize the apparatus. The Gloss and Augustine note the band was Roman, granted by the governor with legal authority to take the supposed criminal and crush resistance; Chrysostom asks how soldiers were persuaded and answers bluntly — “by hiring them; for being soldiers, they were ready to do anything for money.” Theophylact reads the torches as a guard against Christ slipping away in the dark.

The Reformed Calvin turns the great retinue into a portrait of conscience: “so large a retinue is a sign of a bad conscience, which always trembles without any cause,” the chief priests’ men more enraged than the rest “as if they had cared more about religion.” Lightfoot, characteristically, supplies the rabbinic color of the torch-dance at the feast of Tabernacles. The cumulative impression — civil and ecclesiastical power, Gentile and Jew, combined against Christ (so Henry, citing Psalm 2:1–2) — sets the stage for the repulse that follows.
4:
The comment on John 18:4 is brief and unanimous across schools: Christ’s foreknowledge governs the whole scene, and his going forth to meet the band is the act of a willing victim, not a cornered one.

The grammatical-historical voices anchor this in the text. Robertson notes that “knowing all the things that were coming upon him” recalls John 13:1: Jesus “was not taken by surprise,” and “the surrender and death of Jesus were voluntary acts, though the guilt of Judas and the rest remains.” Clarke reads the same readiness — having finished his preaching, his miracles, and the approach of his Passion, “he had nothing to do now but to offer up himself on the cross; he therefore went forth to meet them, to deliver himself up to death.” Ellicott discusses what “went forth” means — most likely out of the garden itself, though it may mean from the depth of the garden, or from the circle of the disciples, or from the shade into the moonlight — and places the kiss of Judas, recorded by the earlier Gospels, between “went forth” and “said unto them,” with Jesus boldly facing the danger and directing it upon himself, “that the disciples may be saved from it” (v.8). Barnes simply cross-references the foreknowledge to John 10:17–18 and Acts 2:28.

The Reformed Calvin and Gill press the theology of that omniscience. Gill grounds it in deity — “as being the omniscient God, his knowledge reaches to all persons and things, without any limitation,” though here it has special regard to all that should befall him. Calvin draws the consequence for power: the verse states “more clearly with what readiness Christ went forward to death,” and at the same time “describes the great power which he exercised by a single word, in order to inform us that wicked men had no power over him, except so far as he gave permission.” Foreknowledge and self-surrender, on every reading, are two sides of one truth: he met his betrayers because he chose to. The fathers, through the Catena, sound the same note: Christ, “knowing all things that should come upon him,” goes out to ask “Whom seek ye?” not in ignorance but to draw the encounter upon himself; and Barnes adds that “in no peril, however sudden, and in no circumstances, however difficult,” did his wisdom and calmness fail him — the question itself a token of his composure and of his care to spare the disciples about to be named in v.8.
5:
On John 18:5 the witnesses concentrate on three things: the band’s answer “Jesus of Nazareth,” the loaded reply “I am he,” and the arresting note that Judas “stood with them.” The reading is shared but the grammatical-historical commentators carry most of the weight.

On the name, Gill observes that they say neither “thee” (for they did not truly know him, their eyes “holden” as the men of Sodom were), nor “Christ” (which they denied), nor “that deceiver” (which would expose their malice), but the common, faintly contemptuous title drawn from his upbringing in Nazareth. Ellicott reads it as “an official declaration of the person” they were charged to apprehend, though many of them knew him well (compare John 7:46, Matthew 26:55). Clarke times the answer after Judas’s kiss, the agreed signal (Matthew 26:48).

The reply “I am he” (egō eimi) draws the deepest comment. Ellicott connects it to John 8:58 and the divine Name; the Puritan Henry makes the link explicit — “I AM… is the glorious name of the blessed God” (Exodus 3:14), “and the honour of that name is justly challenged by the blessed Jesus” — while still hearing it as Christ’s honest, unequivocating confession that refuses to dodge even a name of reproach: “he would by no means allow equivocations.”

The detail that “Judas stood with them” is felt by all as a stab. Robertson’s second past perfect (“was standing”) gives “a vivid picture of Judas in the very act of betraying Jesus”; Ellicott dwells on it as the impression burned into the apostle’s memory — the man who had eaten from his Master’s hand that very night now standing with the officers of the Sanhedrin and the Roman band — and even suggests Judas was specially connected with their falling to the ground. Henry calls him the very portrait of an apostate, “one that changes sides… he that used to stand with those that followed Christ now stood with those that fought against him,” and suspects the word “I am he” was an arrow aimed especially at the traitor’s conscience, for “Christ’s coming… will be more terrible to apostates and betrayers than to sinners of any other class.”
6:
On John 18:6 — the band going backward and falling to the ground at “I am he” — the witnesses agree the scene displays Christ’s power and his restraint, but they divide sharply on whether the cause was miraculous.

The Latin and Reformed voices read it as a true display of divine power. Augustine (via the Catena) marvels: “Without a blow, one word struck, drove back, prostrated a crowd fierce with hatred, terrible with arms. For God was hid in the flesh,” and frames his famous question, “What shall he do when he comes to judge, who did this when he was going to be judged?” Gregory the Great supplies the moral contrast — the reprobate fall backward, not seeing where they fall, whereas the elect fall forward upon their faces in fear and repentance. Calvin makes it a foretaste of the last judgment: he who now stood “as a lamb ready to be sacrificed,” his majesty outwardly gone, laid his armed and courageous enemies prostrate with a single word, a visible token of the alarm the wicked will feel when he ascends his throne — the voice that shall “slay the wicked by the breath of his lips” (Isaiah 11:4; compare 2 Thessalonians 2:8). Clarke likewise hears an echo of Job 4:9, that “by the breath of his nostrils they might have been consumed.”

The grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott resist a miracle. Barnes insists “there is no proof that there was here any miraculous power,” and that to suppose mere physical force “greatly detracts from the moral sublimity of the scene”; the cause was the frank, fearless manner of Jesus, the majesty of his person, and the rebuke of conscience. Ellicott agrees the terror sprang from “the majesty of his person” and from the divine Name reaching Jewish ears — “guilt trembled before the calmness of innocence… man fell to the ground before the presence of God.” Robertson leaves it open, asking whether it was the egō eimi claim of John 8:58, mere embarrassment and confusion, or supernatural power. Wesley marvels they renewed the assault at all after “so sensible an experience both of his power and mercy,” supposing the priests credited the wonder to Beelzebub. The debate over mechanism only sharpens the agreed point: he fell into their hands only because he chose to be taken.
7:
On John 18:7 — Jesus asking a second time “Whom seek ye?” and receiving the same answer — the Reformed voices dominate, reading the soldiers’ persistence as a portrait of judicial hardness, while the grammatical-historical commentators keep the narrative thread.

Gill notes first the mercy: that they rose unhurt shows Christ “always did good, and not hurt, to the bodies of men; he never disabled any, or took away life, or limb,” and that the same divine person who struck them down “suffered them to rise, and gave them power and strength to get up.” Yet their renewed seeking is “a plain proof of that judicial hardness of heart, under which they were,” and a warning that “even miracles wrought will not bring hardened sinners to repentance without powerful and efficacious grace.” Gill adds that Christ put the question again to show he had no design to escape, though he easily could, and was willing to be apprehended. Calvin presses this furthest: the scene shows “the powerful effect of that blindness with which God strikes the minds of wicked men.” Oxen and asses feel it when they fall, “but those men, after having had an open display of the divine power of Christ, proceed as fearlessly as if they had not perceived in him even the shadow of a man; nay, Judas himself remains unmoved.” He traces it to Satan and to the reprobate mind of Romans 1:28, men who “feel his power, indeed, but not so as to be disposed to obey,” harder than stones, “for sooner will they be broken a hundred times than they will yield.”

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson are soberer. Ellicott warns against the popular notion that the men were struck down helpless: “their fear has passed away,” and Christ’s repeated question shows “his thought is still of saving those who are with him” — the band have “no warrant to take any” but Jesus of Nazareth. Robertson notes simply that the soldiers and officers “know who it is, but are still overawed.” The verse thus joins two notes already sounded: divine restraint toward the disciples and divine hardening of the enemy.
8:
On John 18:8 — “If therefore ye seek me, let these go their way” — every school hears the same dominant note: the Good Shepherd shielding his flock even as he surrenders himself.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it as the deliberate, prudent care of the Captain of salvation. Barnes: “he, the faithful Captain of salvation, went foremost into trials; he, the Good Shepherd, secured the safety of the flock, and went before them into danger,” and “the wisdom, caution, and prudence of Jesus forsook him in no peril, however sudden” (citing Isaiah 53:6, Ephesians 5:25); the apostles were to carry news of his death to the ends of the earth, so by his question he secured their safety. Ellicott suggests some of the cohort, not knowing Jesus, may already have been laying hands on the disciples, so that the Shepherd “will shield the flock” from a real and present danger. Robertson connects the solicitude to the warning and prayer of Luke 22:31–32: “Jesus shows solicitude for the eleven… he is trying to help them.” Clarke stresses the tone: these are “words of authority, than words of entreaty” — “I voluntarily give myself up to you, but you must not molest one of these… at your peril injure them.”

The Reformed Calvin makes the same point pastorally: Christ “not only submits to death of his own accord… but also discharges the office of a good Shepherd in protecting his flock.” He sees the wolves attack and “does not wait till they come to the sheep… but immediately goes forward to guard them,” laying down “a rule which shepherds ought to follow.” The Wesleyan Wesley sees in the soldiers’ obedience “an eminent instance of his power over the spirits of men,” that they did not seize even Peter after the blow to Malchus.

One outlier turns the verse polemical: the catechist Cyril of Jerusalem invokes it against the heretic Mani, who fled from prison rather than, like Christ, saying “If ye seek me, let these go their way” — or, like Jonah, “Take me, and cast me into the sea: for this storm is because of me” (Jonah 1:12).
9:
On John 18:9 — “that the saying might be fulfilled… Of those whom thou gavest me I have lost none” — the witnesses agree the reference is to John 17:12, but the schools differ on how a saying about spiritual safety is “fulfilled” in the bodily rescue of the disciples.

The grammatical-historical commentators treat the quotation formula itself. Robertson notes that the regular “that it might be fulfilled” (hina plērōthē), elsewhere used of Scripture, is here applied to the prophecy of Jesus — “John treats the saying of Jesus as on a par with the Old Testament.” Ellicott draws three lessons from the citation: that John quotes the substance, not the exact words, in keeping with the literary habits of the age; that he applies to temporal persecution what was spoken of spiritual persecution, illustrating how words are “fulfilled” in more than one sense, each fulfilment being only part (compare John 2:17; 12:38); and that John’s quoting the prayer of John 17 as a “saying” that is fulfilled proves it is a real record of Christ’s words, “no résumé” but an actual prayer.

The Reformed voices answer the tension theologically. Calvin grants the passage “appears to be inappropriately quoted, as it relates to their souls rather than to their bodies,” then resolves it: Christ, “sparing them for a time, made provision for their eternal salvation.” Knowing how weak they then were — “what do we think they would have done, if they had been brought to the test?” — “he did not choose that they should be tried beyond the strength which he had given to them,” and from this Calvin draws the general doctrine that God “never brings his people into the field of battle till they have been fully trained, so that even in perishing they do not perish.” Barnes simply observes that he who had kept them more than three years “still sought their welfare, even when his death was near.” Bodily preservation, on this reading, is the outward edge of an eternal keeping. The Latin Augustine, in the Catena, raises the sharpest form of the difficulty — were the disciples never to die? how then would he have lost them had they died now? — and answers that they would have been lost because “they did not yet believe in him in a saving way,” their faith too weak to stand the test; so the keeping of John 17:12 reaches, through their bodily rescue, to the rescue of their souls from a fatal apostasy.
10:
John 18:10 — Peter cutting off Malchus’s right ear — draws comment from every school, divided between the grammatical-historical handling of the facts and the fathers’ moral and allegorical reading.

The grammatical-historical commentators note what is distinctive to John: he alone names both the striker (Peter) and the victim (Malchus), and he and Luke alone specify the “right ear.” Barnes, Clarke, Wesley, and Robertson agree on the reason for the silence of the earlier Gospels and John’s freedom — both men were probably alive when the Synoptics were written but long dead by John’s day (compare the peril to Peter hinted at in John 18:26). Robertson glosses the diminutive ōtarion and explains that Peter, alarmed by Christ’s words about his peril, jerked out one of the two swords the eleven carried (Luke 22:38), missing the man’s head as he swerved to his left. Lightfoot adds that “Malchus” (Malluch, Nehemiah 10:4) was a common name. Clarke supposes Peter “designed to have cloven his skull in two,” God turning the blow aside so that only the ear was taken.

The Greek fathers (Chrysostom, Theophylact) ask where a man forbidden even a second tunic got a sword — Chrysostom guesses he foresaw the occasion and provided one; Theophylact, that he kept the knife from the Passover lamb. They distinguish Peter’s defending his Master from avenging himself, and read the miracle (Luke’s detail of the healing) as Christ teaching us to “do good to those who suffer” even from enemies, and as a proof of his power upon one of the very men who came to take him.

The Latin Augustine supplies the allegory: “Malchus” means “about to reign,” so the ear cut off and healed by the Lord figures the abolition of the old hearing “in the oldness of the letter” and the gift of new hearing “in the newness of the Spirit” — the servant’s bondage exchanged for the liberty of those who shall reign with Christ. Theophylact reads the severed ear of the high priest’s servant as a type of the Jews’ deafness, its restoration as their final enlightenment at the coming of Elias. The Reformed Calvin condemns Peter’s “foolish zeal”: courageous, but undertaken “out of his own fancy,” resisting “the power which God has appointed,” so that he “acts the part of a highwayman” and might have lent color to all the calumnies against Christ — a lesson that “our zeal will succeed ill, whenever we venture to undertake any thing contrary to the word of God.”
11:
On John 18:11 — “Put up thy sword… the cup which my Father hath given me, shall I not drink it?” — the witnesses agree the rebuke is gentle and the “cup” is Christ’s death, freely embraced; the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices carry the comment.

The grammatical-historical commentators treat the imagery and its echoes. Robertson and Clarke explain the “cup” as a metaphor for Christ’s death — sometimes “the lot of life, whether prosperous or adverse,” here his final sufferings — the same figure used to James and John (Matthew 20:22) and in the Gethsemane agony (Matthew 26:39; Luke 22:42) which John does not record. Ellicott notes this is the only appearance of the image in John, and hears in “which my Father hath given me” a “tender trustfulness which robs the cup of all its bitterness” — an echo of the unrecorded garden prayer: the Father to whom he prayed, whose presence never leaves him (John 16:32), into whose hands he will commend his spirit (Luke 23:46). Robertson glosses “sheath” (thēkē), more exact than Matthew’s “place.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the ethic. The rebuke rests on a principle gathered from the Synoptic “he who strikes with the sword shall perish by the sword” (Matthew 26:52): a private individual may not rise “against those who had been invested with public authority,” and we must “beware of repelling our enemies by force or violence… except so far as the institutions and laws of the community admit.” Yet Calvin guards against fanatics who forbid seeking remedies for trouble — afflictions are medicinal draughts the Father administers, “a sweet or a bitter draught,” and we may “defend our life by those aids which he has himself appointed,” provided we attempt nothing “but what is permitted by the word of God” (cf. Acts 21:14). Gill adds that Christ’s healing of the ear (Luke) tended greatly to conciliate the band and make them easy. The cup given by the Father becomes the pattern of all Christian submission — “it is but a cup… given us by a Father, who has a Father’s authority, and does us no wrong.”
12:
On John 18:12 — the band, the captain, and the officers seizing and binding Jesus — the grammatical-historical commentators clarify the personnel while the fathers and the Reformed dwell on the meaning of the binding.

The grammatical-historical voices fix the actors. Ellicott and Robertson note the “captain” (chiliarch) is the Roman tribune commanding the cohort — now actually present, not mentioned before (compare Acts 21:31) — distinct from the “officers of the Jews,” the temple servants and apparitors of the Sanhedrin already named in v.3. Robertson glosses the technical verbs for “seized” (synelabon, the arrest word of the Synoptics) and “bound” (edēsan): as a matter of course with the hands behind the back, “but with no warrant in law and with no charge against him,” and he discusses the puzzle of a preliminary inquiry before Annas given only by John, while the Sanhedrin gathered before Caiaphas.

The Latin fathers read the binding. Augustine: “they bound him… by whom they ought to have wished to be loosed,” and supposes some later delivered by Christ cried, “Thou hast broken my chains asunder.” Bede and Alcuin explain the lawless “high priest that year”: the office, once for life and hereditary, had been “abandoned to ambitious men” — Alcuin citing Josephus that Caiaphas bought it, so “no wonder if a wicked high priest judged wickedly.” Chrysostom hears in the leading-away to Annas a triumphal exultation, “as if they were raising a trophy,” yet finds even the enemies echoing truth, for the Evangelist reminds us of Caiaphas’s word that “one man should die for the people” (John 11:50) — “such is the overpowering force of truth, that even its enemies echo it.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the redemptive exchange: “the body of the Son of God was bound, that our souls might be loosed from the cords of sin and of Satan,” and notes the earlier miracle was useful in two ways — to take away the offense, lest we think Christ “yielded as if he had been overcome by weakness,” and to prove “that in dying he was altogether voluntary.” One note is misplaced: the Jamieson-Fausset-Brown entry here comments on Christ’s hunger and the Samaritan woman, not on this arrest.
13:
On John 18:13 — Jesus led to Annas first, father-in-law of Caiaphas the high priest “that same year” — the grammatical-historical commentators reconstruct the office and the man, while the Reformed and the Puritan Henry read providence and corruption in the scene.

The grammatical-historical voices identify Annas. Lightfoot calls him the “sagan” or prefect of the priests, the ruler so often named by the Rabbins, brought in first as the elder of “greater experience and skill in the law,” since some high priests were “very unlearned fellows.” Barnes and Clarke add that Annas had himself long been high priest, had five sons who successively held the office, and his son-in-law held it now — so it was important to secure his sanction and counsel; Clarke thinks him chief of the Sanhedrin, to whom in that office Christ was first brought. They explain “that same year”: the office, by the Law during life, had become almost annual through Roman interference, “a presage of its final period approaching.” The Reformed Calvin and Gill agree — Calvin notes it was “not annual” by right, but that ambition and Roman governors deposed and installed high priests “for money or for favor” (Vitellius deposing Caiaphas for Jonathan, son of Annas), and Gill that Annas’s counsel, interest, and authority were sought to push the prosecution to Christ’s death. The Wesleyan Wesley notes nothing remarkable happened at Annas’s house, which is why the other Evangelists omit it.

The Latin Augustine stresses that Judas earned “damnation for the willfulness of his wickedness,” no praise for the “usefulness of his treachery,” and harmonizes John with Matthew’s shorter notice of Caiaphas (Jesus led to Annas first “simply because he was his father-in-law”). The Puritan Henry frames the whole: they “led him as a lamb to the slaughter,” hurried him in violence through the sheep-gate (Nehemiah 3:1); and Lightfoot’s suggestion that Annas was that morning inspecting the day’s temple sacrifices for blemishes becomes, in Henry, a type — “Christ, the great sacrifice, was presented to him, and sent away bound, as approved and ready for the altar.” Henry reads in Caiaphas being high priest “that same year” a providence: “when a bad thing was to be done by a high priest… Providence so ordered it that a bad man should be in the chair to do it.”
14:
John 18:14 recalls that Caiaphas was the one who had counselled “that it was expedient that one man should die for the people” (John 11:50). The comment is brief and falls into two readings.

The grammatical-historical commentators take the note as a comment on the trial’s injustice. Barnes says it is recalled “to show how little prospect there was that Jesus would have justice done him in the hands of a man who had already pronounced on the case.” Clarke agrees Caiaphas was “an improper person to sit in judgment on Christ, whom he had prejudged and precondemned,” and adds the bitter irony that “Christ must not be treated according to the rules of justice: if he had, he could not have been put to death.” Robertson notes the articular aorist participle (“he which gave counsel”) and the retained present tense of “it was expedient” in indirect assertion, pointing the reader back to the deliberate policy of John 11:50.

The Reformed Calvin reads the providence behind the malice: God “employed the foul mouth of a wicked and treacherous” high priest to utter a true prediction, “just as he guided the tongue of the prophet Balaam, contrary to his wish, so that he was constrained to bless the people, though he desired to curse them” (Numbers 23:7–8). The unjust judge had already, unwittingly, prophesied the saving meaning of the very death he was about to procure — that one should die not merely, as he meant it, for political safety, but for the people in the deepest sense. So the verse both exposes the rigged character of the trial and quietly testifies, against the judge’s own will, to the redemptive necessity of the cross. The Puritan Henry reads the same double sense: the repetition of Caiaphas’s saying shows “what a bad man he was,” governing “by rules of policy, in defiance of the rules of equity,” and what “ill usage Christ was likely to meet” in a court where “his case was adjudged before it was heard”; yet it is also “a testimony to the innocency of our Lord Jesus, from the mouth of one of his worst enemies,” who owned that he fell “a sacrifice to the public good.”
15:
John 18:15 — Peter and “another disciple” following Jesus into the high priest’s court — draws two strands of comment: a long-running debate over who the other disciple was, and a meditation on Peter’s peril.

The chief debate concerns the unnamed disciple. The traditional view, that it is John himself, is held by Chrysostom — who says John omits his own name “out of humility” while signalling that he was inside the hall and could report what passed “with more certainty than the other Evangelists” — and by Ellicott and Robertson, who note John’s habitual reticence and his frequent pairing with Peter (Acts 3:1; 4:13), though Ellicott cautions the identification is “probable, but not… necessarily true.” The Reformed Calvin objects sharply: “What intimacy with a proud high priest could John have, who was a mean fisherman?” — so the man was likely not one of the Twelve but a Jerusalem believer. Clarke leans to Grotius’s guess that it was the host at whose house Jesus had supped, and the Latin Augustine, with characteristic caution, refuses to “decide hastily… on a subject concerning which the Scripture is silent.”

On Peter, the fathers and the Reformed converge. Augustine presses that to deny oneself Christ’s disciple is to deny Christ — “the Lord did not say… thou shalt deny that thou art my disciple, but, thou shalt deny me” — and links this to v.9: had Peter died at that moment, he “must have been lost.” Chrysostom adds the famous note that Providence let Peter fall first so that, pardoned, he might “be less severe to sinners… remembering his own weakness.” Gill and Calvin judge Peter’s very following a fault: Christ had foretold the denial and dismissed him, so it had been “far better for him to groan and pray in some dark corner” than to undertake a duty from which Christ had released him. Lightfoot notes the textual question of whether v.24 belongs here, since the denials seem set in Caiaphas’s hall. Gregory the Great supplies the image: “the fire of love was smothered in Peter’s breast,” warming himself at the coals of the persecutors.
16:
On John 18:16 — Peter left standing at the door until the other disciple speaks to the portress and brings him in — the comment is brief and mostly grammatical-historical, with the Reformed Gill drawing the moral.

Ellicott and Robertson fix the setting. Peter “stood at the door of the court,” the prisoner and his friend (a friend of the high priest) having gone in, while the door-keeper admitted him at the other disciple’s word. Robertson glosses the word for door-keeper (thyrōros, from thyra, door, and a root for care), noting it is masculine in John 10:3 but feminine here, a female porter; he marks again the picturesque periphrastic “was standing” as in v.5. Ellicott documents from Josephus and the Septuagint (2 Samuel 4:6; Acts 12:13) that women kept the door among the Jews.

The Reformed Gill alone draws the lesson: the very difficulty of entry was a providential hint Peter failed to take. He “perhaps might be fearful too of going in, lest he should be known”; but “it would have been well if he had took the hint of providence, access not being easy, and have gone his way.” “He was now at the door of temptation”; it would have been best for him to stay out, “and indeed at a greater distance,” but his curiosity carried him in, hoping for an opportunity of getting nearer. Henry, in his fuller treatment, judges the other disciple’s kindness in introducing Peter to be, “as it proved, no kindness” but a “great diskindness”: by letting him into the high priest’s hall he “let him into temptation,” for “the courtesies of our friends often prove a snare to us, through a misguided affection.” Robertson adds that the other disciple’s being known well enough “for the portress to admit” Peter need not imply a personal friendship with the high priest, and that “the account of what happened to Peter might well seem to be told from the point of view of the servants’ hall.” The narrow door, left unheeded, opens directly on the threefold denial that follows.
17:
On John 18:17 — the door-keeping maid’s “Art not thou also one of this man’s disciples?” and Peter’s “I am not” — the witnesses agree on the smallness of the test and the gravity of the fall, with the Reformed Calvin most eloquent.

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the question. Robertson notes the form (mē kai su) expects the answer “no” — “she made it easy for Peter to say no” — and the contemptuous “this man’s,” with a gesture toward Jesus. Ellicott and Wesley agree there was “no charge brought against him”; the words were words of recognition, or a reason for admitting him with his friend, and “she does not appear to have asked with any design to hurt him.” Yet Ellicott adds the sting: Peter, conscious that he had attempted to kill, and had wounded, one of the high priest’s servants, “dreads this recognition.” Clarke supplies the antiquarian detail that the portress was named (by one writer) Ballila, and that women, especially old women, commonly kept gates among the ancients, citing Aristophanes, Euripides, and Plautus. Gill imagines her curiosity at the man whom one of note had ordered admitted.

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a mirror of human frailty: “when he stumbles so shamefully at the first step, the foolishness of his boasting is exposed”; “at the voice of a single woman, and that voice unaccompanied by threatening, he is confounded and throws down his arms.” All the boasted strength of man “is smoke, which a breath immediately drives away”; we “tremble at the rustling of a falling leaf… let us therefore learn not to be brave in any other than the Lord.” He insists, too, that an evasive denial is still a denial: “when Peter is afraid to acknowledge that he is” a disciple, “it amounts to an assertion that he has nothing to do with him” — a warning against escaping confession “by acting the part of a sophist.” Henry sharpens the shame of so slight a test: had it been Malchus threatening vengeance there were some excuse, “but when a maid only asked him… he might without danger have answered, And what if I am?” — for “those can bear but little for Christ that cannot bear this; this is but running with the footmen,” and “the fear of man brings a snare.”
18:
John 18:18 — the servants and officers warming themselves at a fire of coals, “and Peter stood with them” — is handled mostly by the grammatical-historical commentators, with the Reformed drawing the moral.

Robertson and Ellicott gloss the vivid anthrakian, “a heap of burning coals” (the source of our “anthracite”), a word in the New Testament only here and at the charcoal fire of John 21:9 — a quiet echo between the place of Peter’s denial and the place of his restoration. They distinguish the high priest’s household “servants” from the temple “officers” (as in v.3), and note the Roman soldiers had apparently withdrawn to their barracks. On the cold — “there was coldness,” Robertson renders — Lightfoot offers a traveller’s report (Biddulph, at Jerusalem at Passover) that nights there, though the days were hot, turned sharply cold from heavy dews, even risking frost, so that the Rabbis “do not intercalate the year for snow or for frost.” Ellicott observes that Peter, already identified as a disciple, joined the company round the fire because to stand aloof “would have been to call further attention to himself.”

The Reformed Calvin marks Peter’s “stupidity” in lingering “without the least concern… along with a multitude of wicked men, after having denied his Master,” though he allows fear of fresh danger outside the high priest’s house may have held him there. Gill draws the application: “the company and conversation of wicked men should be abstained from; no good is got thereby… Satan baits his temptations for the fleshly and sensitive part,” so that what Peter thought was for his good “is the occasion of hurt.” Henry presses the company Peter kept: “those that warm themselves with evil doers grow cold towards good people,” and “those that are fond of the devil’s fire-side are in danger of the devil’s fire”; better, he says, to “weep in secret” for his Master than to stand idle among his enemies — “gather not my soul with sinners” (Psalm 26:9). The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here is again misplaced, commenting on Christ’s hunger rather than this scene.
19:
On John 18:19 — the high priest questioning Jesus “of his disciples, and of his doctrine” — the witnesses agree the interrogation was a hunt for a charge, not an honest inquiry.

The grammatical-historical commentators specify the two aims. Barnes: the question about disciples sought to prove “a strong and dangerous faction,” grounding a charge of sedition against Caesar — “an unarmed and despised Galilean” whom they hoped “to draw into confessions which art and malice could use.” The question about doctrine sought teaching “that tended to subvert the Mosaic institutions, or that were treasonable.” Ellicott reads it as a series of questions seeking “some technical evidence in Christ’s own words on which they may support the charges.” Robertson identifies this as Annas’s “preliminary examination,” probing whether Jesus made disciples “as a mere rabbi or as Messiah.” Clarke stresses the illegality: by the Talmud (Sanhedrin), criminal trials could neither commence nor terminate but by day, yet “Christ was judicially interrogated and condemned during the night… all the forms of justice were insulted and outraged” (citing Acts 8:33, “in his humiliation his judgment was taken away”).

The fathers, through the Catena, read the same malice. Chrysostom thinks Annas hoped to brand Jesus “a seditious and factious person whom no one attended to, except his own disciples”; Theophylact, that the question on doctrine sought ground to kill him “as an enemy of God”; Alcuin, that he asked “not in order to know the truth, but to find out some charge against him” for the Roman governor. Augustine wrestles with the apparent tension between “in secret have I said nothing” and Christ’s private teaching of the disciples, resolving that he spoke “openly” in that many heard, though not openly in that many did not understand. The Reformed Calvin sums it: the high priest interrogates him “as if he had been some seditious person… and… a false prophet,” while Christ, having faithfully discharged the office of teacher, enters no new defense but simply “reproves the impudence of the high priest, who inquires about a matter perfectly well known, as if it had been doubtful.”
20:
On John 18:20 — “I spake openly to the world… in secret have I said nothing” — the schools agree this is the answer of conscious innocence, appealing to the public character of Christ’s ministry.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the openness. Robertson catalogues the temple teaching (John 7:14; 8:20) and synagogue teaching (John 6:59), noting Jesus “ignores the sneer at his disciples, but challenges the inquiry about his teaching as needless.” Barnes contrasts him with conspirators who “train their friends in secret” and lay their plans in private; he had proclaimed his views “in every place of public concourse,” teaching nothing to his disciples “which he had not himself taught in public” (Matthew 10:27; Luke 12:3). Ellicott emphasises the strongly emphatic pronoun — “I am one who spake plainly and to all men” — and that his followers were not “initiated into secret mysteries, nor made conspirators in any political organisation.” Clarke argues the “world” here means the Jewish people, since Christ did not preach to the Gentiles, and presses the challenge to Pilate: “call witnesses… and then proceed to judge according to the evidence.” Wesley parses it neatly: openly “as to the manner,” continually “as to the time,” in synagogue and temple “as to the place.”

The Reformed Calvin guards against a misuse: the verse does not condemn those who expound God’s word privately under persecution, for Christ “does not argue as to what is lawful… but… to put down the insolent malice of Caiaphas.” He reconciles it with the charge to proclaim from the housetops (Matthew 10:27) and the mysteries given only to the Twelve (Matthew 13:11): the substance of his doctrine was always the same, the manner of teaching various, so he could “testify with a good conscience” that he had concealed nothing and acted with no cunning. Henry adds that the doctrine of Christ, “purely and plainly preached, needs not be ashamed to appear in the most numerous assembly,” for “wisdom cries in the places of concourse” (Proverbs 1:21), and what Christ’s faithful ministers say “they would be willing all the world should hear” — “truth fears nothing but concealment.”
21:
On John 18:21 — “Why askest thou me? ask them which heard me” — the witnesses agree Christ is claiming the protection of legal procedure, and the comment is almost wholly grammatical-historical.

Ellicott corrects a common misreading: the stress is not on “me” but on the interrogative — “Why, for what purpose, dost thou ask me? If you want witnesses, ask them which heard me” — and notes “these know” probably pointed to officers then present who had heard him (John 7:46). Robertson observes there were “abundant witnesses to be had,” multitudes having heard Jesus only days before in the great temple debate when the Sanhedrin were routed “to the joy of the common people” (Mark 12:37), and that the urgent aorist imperative “ask” calls for instant action. Lightfoot frames it as an appeal to the court’s own rule, that witnesses for the defendant should be heard first — a rule they trampled, seeking only false witness against him (Matthew 26:59) and never truly examining or admonishing the witnesses as their custom required.

Barnes draws the application: though willing to be reviled and persecuted, Jesus “also insisted that justice should be done him”; conscious of innocence, he could “appeal to the vast multitudes” who had heard him, and so Christians may, “when persecuted and reviled, meekly but firmly insist on our rights” — for “laws are made to protect the innocent as well as to condemn the guilty” (1 Peter 4:13–16). The Reformed Gill notes Christ’s surprise that, standing bound as a criminal and the defendant in the cause, he should be made to accuse himself — for he was “not obliged to accuse himself.” Henry hears in the appeal a tacit charge against the judges’ own illegality: “Why ask you me?” implies both “why ask… when you have already condemned” (having decreed excommunication for any who owned him, John 9:22) and “must I accuse myself, when you have no evidence against me?” — so “the doctrine of Christ may safely appeal to all that know it,” and “those who judge in truth bear witness to it.”
22:
John 18:22 — an officer striking Jesus, “Answerest thou the high priest so?” — draws comment on the blow’s injustice, its prophetic fulfilment, and Christ’s meekness; the Latin Augustine gives the richest reflection.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word for the blow (rhapisma, in the New Testament only at Mark 14:65, here, and John 19:3) is itself uncertain — a stroke with a rod or staff of office, or, more likely, “a most insulting” slap in the face (Robertson, Barnes, Ellicott). All press the illegality: a prisoner before condemnation stood under the protection of justice, and Ellicott infers from Acts 23:2 that, since a blow on the face was a customary punishment for an offence against the high priest’s dignity but here came unauthorised from a mere attendant, “this was not a legal trial before the judicial authority.” Clarke agrees that “if justice had been done to Christ, he could neither have suffered nor died.”

The fathers see prophecy and patience. Alcuin hears the fulfilment of “I gave my cheek to the smiters” (Isaiah 50:6; compare “they shall smite the judge of Israel with a rod upon the cheek,” Micah 5:1). Augustine marvels at the answer — “what can be truer, gentler, kinder?” — and resolves the puzzle of why Christ did not literally “turn the other cheek”: he did both, answering gently and giving his whole body to the cross, teaching that the precept of patience is kept “not by posture of the body, but by preparation of the heart,” since “a man might give his cheek outwardly, and yet be angry.” Theophylact paraphrases the rebuke (“if I taught aright… why smite you me?”); Chrysostom marks that the priests, unable to disprove or admit his words, betray that “it is not a trial they are carrying on, but a faction, a tyranny.” Bede explains the binding sent on to Caiaphas (v.24). The Reformed Calvin condemns the “barbarous assembly,” judges “as cruel as ferocious beasts,” from which “not only all justice, but likewise all humanity and modesty, are banished.” (The Cyril of Jerusalem note attached here is a stray fragment on the “Catholic Church,” unrelated to the verse.)
23:
On John 18:23 — Christ’s reply to the blow, “If I have spoken evil, bear witness of the evil; but if well, why smitest thou me?” — the witnesses agree he answered with reasoned meekness, and several address how this squares with his own command to turn the other cheek.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a lawful demand for justice. Ellicott paraphrases: “Produce the evidence which the law requires.” Robertson parses the first-class condition (“assumed to be true” for the sake of argument) — “then prove it, that is all” — and notes plainly that Jesus, “clearly entitled to proof of such a charge,” “did not here turn the other cheek.” Barnes draws the doctrine most fully: the precept of Matthew 5:39 “is consistent with a firm demand that justice should be done us”; it refers “rather to private matters than to judicial proceedings,” and does not require that, when the law is on our side, “we should sacrifice our rights to the malignant accuser,” which would be “giving legal triumph to the wicked” (citing 1 Peter 2:19–23).

The Reformed Calvin and Gill agree. Gill notes Christ replied “without making use of his divine power as the Son of God, or discovering any warmth of spirit… mildly and rationally.” Calvin reads the complaint of a grievous wrong: “if I have sinned, accuse me, that, when the cause has been tried, I may be punished according to the offense; for this is not a lawful mode of procedure.” He then resolves the apparent breach of Matthew 5:39: Christian patience does not always require silence, but means first to bear the injury with patience and second to renounce all thought of revenge (Romans 12:21). It is “a foolish exposition” to think we are commanded to hold out fresh inducements to those already disposed to do mischief; rather, each should be “more ready to bear a second injury than to take revenge for the first,” so “there is nothing to prevent a Christian man from expostulating, when he has been unjustly treated, provided that his mind be free from rancour, and his hand from revenge.”
24:
John 18:24 — “Annas sent him bound to Caiaphas the high priest” — turns almost entirely on a grammatical question, and is handled by the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices alike.

The debate is whether the verb is a plain aorist (“sent”) or carries pluperfect force (“had sent”). The Authorised Version, with Calvin, Clarke, and Wesley, reads it as a pluperfect parenthesis: the verse is a recapitulation that properly belongs after v.13, so that the examination just narrated took place in Caiaphas’s hall, not Annas’s. Calvin states he “preferred translating it… Had Sent, rather than, Sent,” explaining that the Evangelist, having said Christ was taken to Annas and then continued the narrative as if the priests had assembled there, now reminds the reader that he was sent on to the high priest’s house. Clarke notes the proper place of the verse is immediately after the thirteenth, and that one Vienna manuscript and the later Syriac actually move it there. Ellicott and Robertson resist this: the tense “is an aorist, and cannot properly have a pluperfect force,” so “Annas therefore sent him bound” after his own preliminary examination was over — which, Ellicott argues, is the more consistent reading, since it avoids using “high priest” of Annas and Caiaphas indiscriminately (see v.15), though he admits both interpretations “do violence to the ordinary meaning of language” unless his own scheme is followed.

All agree on the one substantive point: Jesus was still bound, as he had been since his arrest (v.12), apparently unbound only during the hearing before Annas. Robertson notes he was “bound on his arrest and apparently unbound during the preliminary examination by Annas,” then bound again. Calvin, for his part, judges the question of order of small consequence — the other Evangelists omit the visit to Annas “because it does not greatly affect the substance of the narrative; for nothing was done there worthy of being recorded” — the convenient nearness of Annas’s house perhaps inducing them to lodge Christ there “till the high priest assembled the council.” The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is again misplaced, commenting on the faith of the Samaritans of John 4.
25:
On John 18:25 — the narrative returning to Peter “standing and warming himself” for his second denial — the witnesses note John’s deliberate interleaving of the denial with Christ’s examination, and the schools divide between harmonizing the accounts and drawing the moral.

The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott mark the repeated periphrastic imperfects (as in v.18), a “vivid renewal of the picture,” placed deliberately so that Peter is denying in the court while Jesus is sent from Annas to Caiaphas, “from one wing of the quadrangular building across the court to the other” (Luke 22:61). Robertson notes each Gospel gives three denials but they cannot be made into an exact parallel, “as probably several people joined in each time,” with an hour’s interval here (Luke 22:59), and that the question is again framed so “that Peter should say ‘No’” — “a favourite device of the devil in making temptation attractive.”

The Latin Augustine labours to reconcile the Evangelists — the “another damsel” of Matthew, the “one” of Luke, and John’s plural “they said” — concluding Peter went out and returned to the fire, and that “the prophecy of the Physician is fulfilled, the presumption of the sick man demonstrated” (“thou shalt deny me thrice”). Chrysostom reads the once-fervid disciple now “too torpid to move even when our Lord was carried away,” showing “how weak man’s nature is, when God forsakes him,” and that the Evangelists record it “to teach us how hurtful it is to trust in self.” The Reformed Calvin marks “how shocking the stupidity of Peter,” who “not only has no feeling of repentance, but hardens himself by the very indulgence he takes in sinning” — first a small fault, then habitual, then conscience laid asleep, so the warning is to be “early on our guard.” Bede stands apart with allegory: the denials and cock-crowings figure those who denied Christ’s deity before and after the resurrection, and the two resurrections; the damsels, lust and carnal delight; the servants, the devils who urge denial. Gill draws the plain application that the verse is “repeated… to connect the history” of Peter’s denial, interrupted by the examination of Christ inserted at the same time, and warns again that the “company and conversation of wicked men” proved “of bad consequence to him” — men being “too often more concerned for their bodies than their souls.”
26:
On John 18:26 — the high priest’s servant, kinsman of Malchus, pressing “Did not I see thee in the garden with him?” — the comment is brief and wholly grammatical-historical, fixing the heightened peril of this third challenge.

Ellicott and Robertson both note that this question, unlike those of v.17 and v.25 (which expected “no”), is framed with ouk to expect an affirmative answer — the kinsman “feels certain that he is not deceived” and “is not to be silenced by a simple denial.” Robertson stresses the danger: Peter, observed by a relative of the very man he had tried to kill (v.10), was now “in dire peril… of arrest himself for attempt to kill,” which drove him to his third denial “with cursing and swearing” (Mark 14:71). Ellicott observes, on the likely reading of v.4 that Jesus went forth out of the garden, that the moment the kinsman saw Peter was probably before Malchus was wounded — for had he witnessed that, “he would almost certainly have charged Peter with it now.” Gill notes the servant pressed the charge “hearing him so stiffly deny that he was a disciple of Jesus, when he had great reason to believe he was,” and being moreover a kinsman of Malchus, which (Robertson and Gill agree) “would make it the more a terror to Peter,” who might now think, “there needs no other witness nor prosecutor.” Robertson adds the Lukan detail that the cock-crowing came about 3 a.m., the close of the third watch (Mark 13:35), that Mark speaks of two crowings (Mark 14:72) “as often happens when one cock crows,” and that Jesus “turned and looked on Peter” (Luke 22:61) as he passed from Annas’s rooms to the trial before Caiaphas and the Sanhedrin — the look that, the witnesses agree, alone broke the apostle’s heart. The whole sequence, Robertson notes, shows how “those who by sin think to help themselves out of trouble do but entangle themselves the more” — “dare to be brave, for truth will out,” since “we are often drawn into sin by groundless causeless fears, which a small degree of wisdom and resolution would make nothing of.”
27:
On John 18:27 — Peter’s third denial and the immediate cock-crow — the witnesses note the fear behind the oath, the harmonizing of the three denials, and the divine warning.

The grammatical-historical Clarke gives the fullest harmony, laying out all three denials by place and time across the Gospels: the first below in the hall of Caiaphas’s house among the servants at the fire; the second in the vestibule about the first cock-crowing, soon after midnight, when another girl charged him; the third about an hour later on the charge of being a Galilean (Luke 22:59), supported by Malchus’s kinsman, when Jesus was in the same room and “turned and looked on Peter.” He argues the apparent conflict between Matthew’s setting the denials in Caiaphas’s house and John’s in Annas’s arises from “the injudicious insertion of the particle” (“therefore”) in v.24, which Griesbach and others omit. Ellicott corrects the rendering to “a cock crew.”

The Reformed Gill explains the vehemence: Peter swore “in a profane manner, and imprecates the judgments of God upon him,” for fear that, recognised as the man who cut off Malchus’s ear, he would face a heavy fine (he reckons four hundred zuzim, “twelve pounds ten shillings”) or some capital punishment. Calvin draws the lesson that the sign alone availed nothing: though warned by God at the very moment of the crowing, “no voice, no sign, no warning, will bring him back, until the Lord himself cast his eyes upon him” (Luke 22:61); the mere crowing did not move him “till Christ” looked. The crowing fulfils the Lord’s prophecy of the threefold denial (John 13:38). Henry, in his fuller treatment of the denials, reads the deepening fall as the nature of sin itself: “the heart is hardened by the deceitfulness of it” (Hebrews 3:13), and the sin of lying especially is “a fruitful sin… one lie needs another to support it, and that another” — “the beginning of sin is as the letting forth of water,” so that, the fence once broken, men “easily go from bad to worse.”
28:
John 18:28 — Jesus led to Pilate’s praetorium while the Jews stay outside “lest they should be defiled, but that they might eat the passover” — draws two great strands: a chronological debate over “the passover,” and a near-unanimous rebuke of hypocrisy.

On the chronology, the grammatical-historical commentators labour to keep John in harmony with the Synoptics. Lightfoot, Barnes, and Robertson argue “the passover” here is not the paschal lamb (eaten the night before) but the festival sacrifices, the Chagigah, of the fifteenth day, the “passover-bullocks” of Deuteronomy 16:2 and 2 Chronicles 30:24; Robertson notes that in John’s eight other uses, and in 2 Chronicles 30:22, “passover” means the feast, not the supper, “which keeps John in harmony with the Synoptics.” Against this, Clarke leans to the view that the paschal lamb is meant, so that Christ either ate it some hours early or was slain at the very hour the lambs were killed — a reading the Catena’s Chrysostom shares (“he himself celebrated it one day before, reserving his own death for the… day on which the old passover was kept”), while Alcuin distinguishes the lamb of the fourteenth from “the great festival… on the fifteenth day.”

On the hypocrisy the witnesses speak with one voice. Augustine cries, “O impious blindness! They feared to be defiled by the judgment hall of a foreign prefect, to shed the blood of an innocent brother they feared not.” Barnes marvels at “petty regard to the mere ceremonies of the law” alongside “the most enormous of all crimes,” men keeping conscience easy “by mere sanctimoniousness.” The Puritan Henry: they “strained at a gnat, and swallowed a camel,” scrupling a traditional, not even a legal, pollution while breaking “all the laws of equity to persecute Christ to the death.” Calvin presses two faults of hypocrisy — that they do not consider they “carry more pollution within their hearts” than any place could give, and that they “carry to excess their care about smaller matters, and neglect what is of the highest importance,” preferring “the traditions of men to the holy commandments of God” (Titus 1:15; Matthew 23:24). Robertson and Clarke add the legal note: the Sanhedrin no longer had the power of death, and there were two violations of Jewish procedure — a capital trial at night and condemnation the same day.
29:
On John 18:29 — Pilate going out to the accusers and demanding “What accusation bring ye against this man?” — the comment is brief, shared between the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices, and unusually favourable to Pilate’s conduct here.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson note Pilate “went out outside” (both ex and exō) because the Sanhedrin would not enter, apparently onto a gallery over the pavement in front of the palace (John 19:13). They mark katēgoria as the formal legal charge, a word rare in the New Testament (also Titus 1:6): Pilate “knew by hearsay what this was, but demands the legal accusation without which the trial could not proceed” — “a proper legal inquiry,” says Robertson — for they had expected him simply to order execution. As the Roman procurator, Ellicott notes, he asks “what crime Jesus has committed against the Roman law.”

Clarke and Gill read Pilate’s going out as condescension to their scruples, the Romans having confirmed to the Jews “the free use of all their rites and ceremonies,” so that “the governor could not do less than comply,” sparing them the defilement of entering. The Reformed Calvin grants that this heathen “is not unwilling to encourage a superstition which he ridicules and despises,” yet “in the main point of the cause… performs the duty of a good judge” in ordering them, “if they have any accusation, to bring it forward” — to which the priests, lacking authority to condemn, “make no other reply than that he ought to abide by their previous decision.” Henry finds in Pilate, “that we may give every one his due,” three things commendable here as a magistrate: “his diligent and close application to business” in rising early to the judgment-seat; “his condescending to the humour of the people” in coming out to them; and “his adherence to the rule of justice, in demanding the accusation,” for it was “a law of nature… that no man should be condemned unheard” (compare Acts 25:16–17) — “it is unreasonable to commit a man without alleging some cause… and much more to arraign a man when there is no bill of indictment found against him.”
30:
On John 18:30 — the priests’ answer, “If he were not a malefactor, we would not have delivered him up unto thee” — the witnesses agree this is an insolent evasion: they bring no charge but demand that Pilate ratify their verdict.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the move. Ellicott: they “take the position that the Roman is the executive, and their own the judicial power,” bringing “no legal charge” but treating their own condemnation as “in itself sufficient proof that he was worthy of death,” using the vague word “malefactor” though in the trial before Caiaphas they had sought to prove no evil deed. Robertson parses the second-class condition (“assumed to be untrue”) as “a pious pose of infallibility not in the Synoptics,” an “insolent reply to Pilate,” and notes the variant reading kakopoios / the participle “doing evil” that emphasises Jesus as a supposed habitual evil-doer. Barnes observes that Pilate, contrary to their expectation that he would “at once give sentence,” “proceeded himself to rehear and retry the cause,” being prepossessed with Christ’s innocence and knowing they had delivered him “for mere envy” (Matthew 27:18). Clarke: they wished to make Pilate “not the judge, but the executor of the sentence which they had already illegally passed.”

The Reformed Calvin reads their pride: “they indirectly complain… that he has not a proper reliance on their integrity,” intoxicated by their own greatness — “see how pride fills people with a sort of drunkenness, and puts them out of their senses.” He sets their charge against the facts: what evil had Christ done “except that he has cured every kind of diseases, has driven the devils out of men… has restored sight to the blind… and life to the dead?” — deeds “those men knew well,” but pride blinds men so that “nothing is more difficult than to arouse them to form a sound and correct judgment.” Henry reads the same insolence and presumption: the priests are “very rude and uncivil to Pilate… very spiteful and malicious towards our Lord Jesus,” resolving “right or wrong” that he be treated as a malefactor — though “he an evil-doer who went about doing good!” — and “very proud and conceited of themselves,” thinking their bare delivering him up “sufficient for the civil magistrate to ground a judicial sentence upon.” (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is again misplaced, on the nobleman of John 4.)
31:
On John 18:31 — Pilate’s “Take ye him, and judge him according to your law,” and the reply “It is not lawful for us to put any man to death” — the witnesses agree on a famous historical point and divide on its exact scope.

The grammatical-historical commentators read Pilate’s words as shrewd or ironic. Robertson says he “shrewdly turns the case over to the Sanhedrin” in reply to their insolence, drawing out at once the admission that “they wanted the death of Jesus, not a fair trial for him, but Pilate’s approval of their purpose to kill him.” All note that capital power had been taken from the Jews — Ellicott and Wesley date it to the deposing of Archelaus when Judaea became a province (A.D. 6–7), the Talmud reckoning it “forty years or more before the destruction of Jerusalem.” Lightfoot devotes a long rabbinic excursus to how they lost it, arguing it lapsed partly through their own laxity, the Sanhedrin removing from the chamber Gazith so that capital judgment fell “as it were out of their hands.” Ellicott observes their words “verbally give up the power, but in reality claim it, and regard the procurator as their executioner.”

The chief debate is whether they retained any power of death. Barnes and Clarke hold they likely kept it in purely religious cases (blasphemy, by stoning — as in the illegal death of Stephen, Acts 7:59–60) but not in matters of sedition or treason against Rome; hence, Clarke argues, they deliberately framed Christ as “an enemy to the Roman government” (Luke 23:2; John 19:12) to force Pilate’s hand — though they elsewhere assert he should die by their law for making himself the Son of God (John 19:7).

The Reformed Calvin reads Pilate as reproaching their “barbarous and violent proceedings” ironically — as if to say, “were he in your power, he would instantly be executed, without being heard… is this the equity of your Law, to condemn a man without any crime?” Behind it all, several hear Genesis 49:10: the sceptre had departed from Judah, a sign that the Messiah was come.
32:
John 18:32 — “that the saying of Jesus might be fulfilled, signifying what death he should die” — draws unanimous comment that the loss of Jewish capital power served, providentially, to bring about crucifixion rather than stoning.

The grammatical-historical commentators make the point precisely. Barnes: had the Jews killed him in the way first contemplated (Matthew 26:4) it would not have been by the Gentiles, nor by crucifixion, which was “particularly a Roman punishment”; so “in the providence of God” the prediction was fulfilled in both respects. Ellicott adds that had the Jews held the power they would have condemned him for blasphemy, punished by stoning (as attempted in John 8:59; 10:31); his being executed “by Roman authority and on a charge of Majestas” (high treason) fulfilled his own word. Robertson notes the fulfilment formula (hina plērōthē) here treats Jesus’ saying as Scripture, as in v.9, the qualitative “by what manner of death” pointing back to the lifting-up sayings of John 3:14 and 12:32. Clarke and Wesley agree crucifixion was Roman, not Jewish, so “had he not been condemned by the Roman governor, he could not have been crucified.”

The Reformed Calvin lifts the eye higher: the chief point in reading the Passion is “to consider the eternal purpose of God.” The Son of God is set before a mortal man’s tribunal; if we suppose this done “by the caprice of men, and do not raise our eyes to God, our faith must necessarily be confounded and put to shame.” But seeing that “by the condemnation of Christ, our condemnation before God is blotted out, because it pleased the Heavenly Father to take this method of reconciling mankind,” we may “boldly, and without shame, glory even in Christ’s ignominy” (Matthew 20:19). Let us, he says, “turn our eyes to God as the Author of our redemption.” Henry adds that even those who “designed the defeating of Christ’s sayings were, beyond their intention, made serviceable to the fulfilling of them by an overruling hand of God”; had the Jews judged him by their own law he had been stoned, “but crucifying never was used among the Jews,” so it was “necessary that Christ should be put to death by the Romans, that, being hanged upon a tree, he might be made a curse for us” (Galatians 3:13).
33:
On John 18:33 — Pilate calling Jesus into the praetorium and asking “Art thou the King of the Jews?” — the witnesses agree this was a private examination prompted by the political charge, and that Pilate already suspected the prisoner’s innocence.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting. Robertson and Ellicott note the hearing was effectively private, since the Jews would not enter (v.28), so Pilate summoned Jesus, who was already inside the court, to his presence. The question of kingship “was the vital problem,” and all four Gospels record it, though only Luke gives the specific accusation that he forbade tribute and called himself a king (Luke 23:2). Robertson observes the emphatic “Thou”: Jesus did claim to be Israel’s spiritual king (John 1:49; 12:13), but the Sanhedrin wished Pilate to understand it “in a civil sense as a rival of Caesar.”

The fathers, through the Catena, read Pilate’s motive. Theophylact says he took Jesus apart “because he had a strong suspicion that he was innocent” and could examine him “more accurately, away from the crowd”; Chrysostom, that Pilate “wishing to rescue him from the hatred of the Jews, protracted the trial,” and with the Jews bringing no charge “began to examine him himself.” Augustine notes the Lord knew both his own question and Pilate’s answer, “but wished it to be written down for our sakes.” The Reformed Calvin frames the whole as Pilate’s “laborious inquiry whether Christ was justly or unjustly accused,” taking up the most offensive charge first that, disposing of it, “he may acquit the prisoner”; Christ, “in order that he may obey his Father, presents himself to be condemned, and this is the reason why he is so sparing in his replies.” The Puritan Henry observes Christ “never took upon him any earthly power… never were any traitorous principles or practices laid to him,” and that Pilate, “entering into judgment with him, that God might not enter into judgment with us,” arraigned as a criminal the One “made sin and a curse for us.” Henry also weighs Pilate’s scornful tone — “What! art thou a king, who makest so mean a figure?” — since he “was far from imagining that really he was so,” yet would “constrain him to say it… that he might proceed upon his own confession.”
34:
On John 18:34 — Christ’s counter-question, “Sayest thou this thing of thyself, or did others tell it thee of me?” — the comment is brief and wholly grammatical-historical, dividing on its purpose.

Several read it as a demand for fair process. Barnes paraphrases: in all your time as praetor, “have you seen anything in me that has led you to apprehend sedition or danger to the Roman power?” — a reminder “that nothing was proved against him,” cautioning Pilate “against being influenced by the malicious accusations of others.” Jesus, he says, “demanded a just trial, and claimed that Pilate should not be influenced by any reports.” Clarke and Gill take it the same way: is the question from Pilate’s own suspicion or observation, or merely from his enemies’ suggestion?

Ellicott offers the deeper reading, that the question establishes a distinction between two senses of the title. “In the political sense in which Pilate would use it, and in this sense only the claim could be brought against him in Roman law, he was not King of the Jews. In the theocratic sense in which a Jew would use that title, he was King of the Jews.” Christ will neither deny the title outright nor let it be understood in Caesar’s sense; so he asks Pilate to say in which sense he means it. Robertson simply notes the ambiguity — whether “a sincere inquiry on Pilate’s part or a trap from the Sanhedrin” — the answer Christ gives turning on which Pilate intends. Henry develops the counter-question into a quiet challenge to the judge: it is “plain that thou hast no reason to say this of thyself,” for nothing in Christ’s conduct ever endangered the Roman government; and “if others tell it thee of me… thou oughtest to consider who they are, and upon what principles they go,” since those who “represent me as an enemy to Caesar are not really such themselves,” using the charge “to cover their malice.” The question is no evasion but a summons to Pilate to weigh his ground — for, Henry adds, had the governor “been as inquisitive as he ought,” he would have found the priests’ true grievance was that Christ “did not set up a temporal kingdom in opposition to the Roman power.”
35:
On John 18:35 — Pilate’s “Am I a Jew? Thine own nation and the chief priests have delivered thee unto me: what hast thou done?” — the witnesses agree it expresses Roman scorn, and the comment is entirely grammatical-historical.

Robertson hears “proud and fine scorn” at the very idea that Pilate had a personal interest in the question — “the gulf between Jew and Gentile yawns wide here” — with vehement negation implied. Ellicott agrees the procurator’s “Roman pride is fired at the very thought”: he was the governor of a subject race, and asks, in effect, “You surely do not suppose that I am a Jew?” caring nothing for their “subtleties and distinctions.” Gill and Clarke draw the inference Pilate intends: since he is no Jew and knows nothing of their King Messiah, his question must have arisen not of himself “but from some intimations the Jews had given him.”

All note the pivot to “What hast thou done?” — a blunt, curt question (Robertson: “What is thy real crime?”) by which, weary of the technicalities “with which a Roman trial had nothing to do,” Pilate presses for the substance. Barnes observes that Pilate, while admitting the charge came from others, still tells Jesus it was “worthy of the deepest attention,” coming from his own nation and its highest authority, and so “besought him to tell him what he had done.” The accusers’ delivering him up is itself, by Acts 3:13, charged to them as their guilt; and Clarke notes the question presses, “If thou dost not profess thyself king… and an enemy to Caesar, what is it that thou hast done?” Henry hears in Pilate’s disdainful “Am I a Jew?” a backhanded testimony to the nation’s disgrace — the Jews, having “corrupted the covenant of their God,” were made “contemptible and base before all the people” (Malachi 2:8–9), so that a Roman “reckoned it a scandal to be counted a Jew”; thus, he notes, “good names often suffer for the sake of the bad men that wear them” — even as Christ “still suffers by those that are of his own nation… that profess relation to him, but do not live up to their profession.”
36:
John 18:36 — “My kingdom is not of this world… else would my servants fight” — is one of the great verses of the chapter, and the witnesses agree it both admits Christ’s kingship and denies that it rivals Caesar.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the negative meaning of “of this world.” Ellicott: it means “the nature and origin of his kingdom are not of this world, not that his kingdom will not extend in this world” (compare John 8:23; 10:16); the proof that he was no political king was that “he stood bound in the presence of the procurator,” his servants not fighting (the one who drew a sword, v.10, having been checked). He warns against pressing “but now” into a millennial sense. Robertson notes “my servants” (the temple-guard’s opposite) were a small despised band who could not war on Caesar — though perhaps Christ alludes also to legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) — and adds “Christians should never forget the profound truth stated here.” Barnes: an earthly kingdom is “defended by arms… engaged in wars,” but Christ’s dominion is “over the heart, subduing evil passions.” Clarke cites Eusebius that the Lord’s relatives, examined by Domitian about the kingdom, called it “altogether heavenly and angelical.”

The fathers (via the Catena) and the Reformed dwell on “not from hence.” Augustine carefully distinguishes: Christ does not say “not here,” for his kingdom is here to the end of the world, with tares among the wheat till harvest, “but it is not from here, since it is a stranger in the world”; all born again in Christ are made “a kingdom not of this world.” Calvin draws its permanence: were it earthly “it would be frail and changeable, because the fashion of this world passeth away” (1 Corinthians 7:31), “but now, since it is heavenly, this assures us of its perpetuity.” He then raises whether the kingdom may be defended by arms, answering that godly princes may “accidentally” defend the Church by discipline and protection, yet “the kingdom of Christ, being spiritual, must be founded on the doctrine and power of the Spirit,” and “is strengthened more by the blood of the martyrs than by the aid of arms” (Luke 17:21).
37:
On John 18:37 — “Art thou a king then?… To this end was I born… that I should bear witness unto the truth” — the witnesses agree Christ here openly confesses his kingship, defined as a reign of truth.

The grammatical-historical commentators parse the confession. Robertson notes the ironical “Art thou a king then?” expects an affirmative, and that “Thou sayest that” means “Yes, because I am a king,” the perfect “have I been born” pointing to the Incarnation as the very purpose — the “good confession” Paul recalls in 1 Timothy 6:13.
