Ellicott argues the two clauses are not to be split (“born to be king… came to bear witness”) but joined: “to be a king came I… in order that I may bear witness unto the truth” — a kingdom that “possessed neither land nor treasury, neither senate nor legions… but was to extend its sceptre over all the kingdoms of the earth.” Barnes presses the point that Christ’s sole power is truth applied to the mind: “men are not forced or compelled to be Christians,” a standing rebuke to persecutors who “by force and flames” would make men think alike (compare Proverbs 23:23). Wesley adds: “every sincere lover of truth will hear him, so as to understand and practise what he saith.”

The Greek Chrysostom marks the contrast: silent under the charge of being a malefactor, Christ speaks freely of his kingdom, “instructing” Pilate and “leading him in to higher matters,” for “to bear witness to the truth shows that he had no crafty purpose.” The Latin Augustine reads “every one that is of the truth heareth my voice” as grace: a man “is not of the truth because he hears his voice, but hears his voice because he is of the truth,” for this grace is “conferred upon him by the truth.” The Reformed Calvin underlines the solemn affirmation, that to speak truth is natural to Christ and his “peculiar office” as one sent by the Father, so “there is no danger… that we shall be deceived by trusting him” (John 8:47).
38:
John 18:38 — Pilate’s “What is truth?” and his verdict “I find in him no fault at all” — draws a notable debate over the tone of the question and a unanimous note of Christ’s declared innocence.

On the question, the grammatical-historical commentators canvass the options. Ellicott lists them: Bacon’s “jesting Pilate” who “would not stay for an answer”; the bitterness of a mind “tossed to and fro in the troubled sea of contemporaneous thought”; sarcasm (“is the son of Roman freedom and Greek thought to learn truth of a Jewish enthusiast?”); or the earnest inquirer of the older interpreters — concluding Pilate was “irritated” by the refusal of a formal charge and thought the matter irrelevant. Barnes and Clarke read contempt: the question was “probably asked in contempt,” and so Jesus did not answer; had Pilate sincerely sought it, as Nicodemus did (John 3:1), Christ “would not have hesitated to explain” — for “all might find truth if they would seek it.” Robertson hears a “famous sneer… as he stood before Incarnate Truth” (John 14:6), from which Pilate “turned with indifference.”

The Reformed Calvin agrees it is “an expression of disdain” — Pilate “thought himself highly insulted” at being represented as ignorant — yet finds even here a flicker of the gospel’s power: “wicked men never reject the doctrine of the Gospel so spitefully as not to be somewhat moved by its efficacy,” Pilate constrained to “feel some inward compunction.” The fathers (Catena) read Pilate going out at once, recalling the Passover custom by which he hoped to save Jesus. All mark the verdict: Pilate “found no fault,” and so, says Robertson, “should have set Jesus free at once” (Luke 23:4). Henry weighs this solemn acquittal: it was “for the justification and honour of the Lord Jesus,” proof that “though he was treated as the worst of malefactors he had never merited such treatment,” and that he “died as a sacrifice for our sins” — the innocent One “cut off, but not for himself” (Daniel 9:26) — while it “aggravated the sin of the Jews” who still thirsted for the blood of one their own judge had cleared.
39:
On John 18:39 — Pilate’s offer to release a prisoner at the Passover, “Will ye therefore that I release unto you the King of the Jews?” — the witnesses agree the custom was a non-legal expedient and the title was spoken in irony.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the custom is found in all the Gospels (Matthew 27:15; Mark 15:6) but its origin is unknown; Gill and Clarke call it a custom, not a law of God or man, of uncertain and perhaps recent date, introduced to ingratiate the governors with the people. Robertson glosses the rare word for “custom” (synētheia, “intimacy, intercourse”), notes the asyndeton in “Will ye that I release?” and hears “contempt and irony in Pilate’s use of the phrase ‘the king of the Jews.’” Ellicott reads the mockery aimed not at Jesus, who was still inside the palace, but at the people: “Such is your national subjection, that he is bound in the praetorium… Shall I release him unto you?”

The Reformed Calvin sees Pilate “all along pondering in what way he might save Christ’s life,” attempting a “middle path” to allay the people’s fury — selecting the notorious Barabbas so that “by a comparison with that man, the hatred which they bore to Christ might be softened down.” Yet he judges the custom itself “a base and heinous crime,” done ostensibly “to honor the sacredness of the day” but in reality “a shameful profanation of it,” since Scripture declares that “he who acquitteth the guilty is abomination in the sight of God” (Proverbs 17:15). Let us learn, he says, that “nothing is more ridiculous than to attempt to serve God by our inventions,” the rule for worship being taken “from nothing else than from his own appointment.” Henry adds that Pilate, hearing how Jesus had lately been hailed with hosannas, “looked upon him as the darling of the multitude, and the envy only of the rulers,” and so expected the people to demand his release; but in naming “an innocent person to be competitor with a notorious criminal” the judge “was willing to trim the matter, and please all sides, being governed more by worldly wisdom than by the rules of equity.”
40:
John 18:40 — the cry “Not this man, but Barabbas” with the stark note “now Barabbas was a robber” — closes the chapter, and the witnesses read it as the tragic, fitting climax of the people’s choice.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the man and the moment. Robertson and Ellicott note that lēstēs (“robber,” a brigand of “unrestrained violence, which often leads to bloodshed”) is sharpened by Luke into insurgent and murderer (Luke 23:19); Robertson adds that the priests put the crowd up to this choice (Mark 15:11), that the name Barabbas means “son of a father,” and that “apparently Jesus died on the very cross planned for Barabbas.” Ellicott marks the “solemn emphasis given to the context by the abrupt brevity of the sentence” (compare Acts 3:14, “ye denied the Holy One… and desired a murderer”). Lightfoot here appends his long excursus on how and when the Jews lost the power of capital judgment. Clarke draws the moral: “there never existed a more… murderous people… no wonder they preferred a murderer to the Prince of peace” — for “like cleaves to like,” and the vilest things are still preferred to Christ.

The Reformed Calvin (commenting at the choice) finds a providence: that the Jews preferred Barabbas “did not happen without a singular interposition of the providence of God,” for it would have been “highly unbecoming that the Son of God should be rescued from death by so dishonorable a price.” He “had taken upon himself the sins of all, which could not be expiated in any other way”; so the guilty man went free while the innocent was condemned — and the glory of the resurrection soon turned that death itself into “a splendid triumph.” Henry presses the folly and fierceness of the choice: the people “cried out… in a heat… with clamour and noise,” for “the enemies of Christ’s holy religion cry it down, and so hope to run it down” (compare the uproar at Acts 19:34); and in choosing a robber over the Saviour they showed how “sin is a robber… and yet foolishly chosen rather than Christ, who would truly enrich us.”
